skill.name: web-security-testing
skill.description: Web application security testing workflow for OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities including injection, XSS, authentication flaws, and access control issues.
skill.summary: Structured workflow for testing web applications against OWASP Top 10 vulnerabilities using phase-based approach.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged 33 potential issues (31 external_commands, 2 weak cryptographic algorithms). After evaluation, all findings are FALSE POSITIVES. The external_commands detections are markdown code formatting (backticks) used for skill references like @scanning-tools, not actual shell execution. The cryptographic flags are false positives from keywords in the OWASP checklist. This is a legitimate security testing workflow with no malicious code.

high_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detection
high_findings.description: Scanner flagged lines 3 and 164 for weak cryptographic algorithm. Line 3 is YAML frontmatter description. Line 164 is OWASP category 'A04: Insecure Design'. No cryptographic code present.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: False positive - scanner incorrectly detected keywords 'insecure' and 'design' as cryptographic weakness. No cryptographic algorithms are used in this file.

medium_findings.title: External Commands Detection
medium_findings.description: Scanner flagged 31 instances of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' at various lines. These are markdown inline code formatting (backticks) used for skill references like `@scanning-tools`, not shell commands.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: False positive - backtick characters in markdown are used for inline code formatting (e.g., `scanning-tools`). These are skill references, not shell command executions.

content.user_title: Test Web Apps for OWASP Top 10 Vulnerabilities
content.value_statement: This workflow guides you through comprehensive security testing of web applications following the OWASP Top 10 methodology, from reconnaissance to reporting.
actual_capabilities: Execute phase-based security testing workflow following OWASP methodology
Test for injection vulnerabilities including SQL, NoSQL, and command injection
Perform XSS testing across reflected, stored, and DOM-based variants
Evaluate authentication mechanisms and session management security
Check access control implementations and privilege escalation vectors
Verify security header configurations and deployment settings
limitations: Does not execute actual exploits - provides guidance only
Requires user to invoke referenced skills for specific testing
Does not include payload generation or fuzzing tools
No actual scanning or tool execution occurs within this skill

use_cases.title: Comprehensive Security Assessment
use_cases.description: Conduct a full security audit of a web application following structured OWASP Top 10 methodology with detailed phase-by-phase testing.

use_cases.title: Bug Bounty Reconnaissance
use_cases.description: Use the workflow for bug bounty hunting to systematically test target applications for vulnerabilities in a structured manner.
use_cases.target_user: Bug bounty hunters and ethical hackers

use_cases.title: Security Validation
use_cases.description: Validate that security controls are properly implemented in a web application before production deployment.

prompt_templates.title: Start Security Test
prompt_templates.prompt: Use @web-security-testing to test my web application for security vulnerabilities. Target: [URL]
prompt_templates.scenario: Begin a new security testing engagement

prompt_templates.title: Test for Injection
prompt_templates.prompt: We are in Phase 2 of @web-security-testing. Test for SQL injection on the login form at [URL] with parameter [param]

prompt_templates.title: XSS Assessment
prompt_templates.prompt: Following Phase 3 of @web-security-testing, test for XSS vulnerabilities in the comment section at [URL]
prompt_templates.scenario: Conduct XSS testing on specific feature

prompt_templates.title: Complete Security Report
prompt_templates.prompt: We have completed all phases of @web-security-testing. Generate a security report summarizing findings and remediation steps.

output_examples.input: Use @web-security-testing to test my web application at https://example.com
output: Ready to proceed to Phase 2: Injection Testing

output_examples.input: We are in Phase 3 of @web-security-testing. Test for XSS in the search feature.
output: Document all successful bypasses with proof of concept

best_practices: Always obtain proper authorization before testing any application
Follow the workflow phases in order for comprehensive coverage
Document all findings with proof of concept for each vulnerability
Invoke referenced skills for specialized testing in each phase
anti_patterns: Skipping phases - each phase builds on previous reconnaissance
Not documenting findings with reproduction steps
Ignoring low-severity findings without proper risk assessment

faq.question: Does this skill execute actual exploits?
faq.answer: No. This is a workflow guidance skill that provides testing methodology and prompts. It does not execute exploits or run tools directly.

faq.question: Do I need other skills to use this workflow?
faq.answer: Yes. This workflow references other skills like @scanning-tools, @sql-injection-testing, @xss-html-injection, and @broken-authentication for specific testing phases.

faq.question: Is this suitable for production testing?
faq.answer: Only with proper written authorization. Always ensure you have explicit permission before testing any web application.

faq.question: What OWASP categories are covered?
faq.answer: All OWASP Top 10 2021 categories are covered including injection, broken authentication, sensitive data exposure, XML external entities, broken access control, security misconfiguration, cross-site scripting, insecure deserialization, using vulnerable components, and insufficient logging.

faq.question: Can I customize this workflow?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflow phases can be adapted based on your target application and scope. Add or modify phases as needed for your assessment.

faq.question: What output format should I use for reports?
faq.answer: Follow the reporting phase guidance to document vulnerabilities with severity, proof of concept, and remediation steps. Use industry standard formats.
